coredata.dc:title: Ball mill-hydrothermal method for one-step synthesis of zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO-CQD) nanocomposites as photocatalyst for degradation of organic pollutants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Bazazi, Sina
Jodeyri, Saeedeh
Hosseini, Seyedeh Parisa
Arsalani, Nasser
Rashidzadeh, Bahaaldin
Fathalipour, Soghra
Seidi, Farzad
Hashemi, Esmail
coredata.dc:description: In this work, we report a cost-effective, green, one-step, and new method without any extra materials to prepare ZnO/CQD photocatalyst. Hence, ZnO nanoparticles and zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (Z-CQD) nanocomposites were synthesized using zinc salt, KOH, and sucrose through the new green ball mill-hydrothermal method. The prepared nanomaterials were characterized by FT-IR, TEM, XRD, FE-SEM, TGA, and UV–Vis absorption. The analysis of the TGA result shows the presence of around 6 wt% of CQD in Z-CQD nanocomposite. The presence of CQD in ZnO nanoparticles decreased the bandgap. The photocatalytic performance of ZnO and Z-CQD were compared by evaluating the degradation of phenazopyridine (PhP), a drug pollutant, and rhodamine B (RhB), an organic dye pollutant, under UV light. The results showed that the degradation rate of RhB is higher than that of PhP. In addition, in the case of RhB, the degradation rate by Z-CQD is higher than that of pure ZnO. Therefore, the presence of carbon quantum dots showed a positive effect on the rate of photocatalytic degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ball Mill-Hydrothermal method
Nanocomposite
Carbon quantum dots
Zinc Oxide
Photocatalyst

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesize of zinc oxide–carbon quantum dots nanocomposites (Z-CQD)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Nanjing Forestry University , Nanjing 210037, China, and the University of Tabriz, Iran.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The proposed mechanism for the formation of Z-CQD nanocomposites.
FT-IR spectra of as-prepared ZnO, Z-CQD 5%, Z-CQD 10%, and Z-CQD 20% nanocomposites.
XRD patterns of as-prepared ZnO, Z-CQD 5%, Z-CQD10%, Z-CQD 15%, and Z-CQD 20%.
XRD patterns of Z-CQD 20% nanocomposite from 2θ = 20 to 2θ = 24.
FE-SEM images of ZnO and Z-CQD 20%.
EDX spectrum of ZnO and Z-CQD 20%.
TEM images of ZnO and Z-CQD 20%.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of as-prepared ZnO, Z-CQD 5%, Z-CQD 10%, Z-CQD 15%, and Z-CQD 20%.
TGA analysis of pure ZnO and Z-CQD 20% nanocomposite in the range of 50 to 800 °C.
UV–Vis. Absorption of RhB and PhP in presence of as-prepared photocatalysts.
Degradation diagram of RhB and PhP in presence of as-prepared photocatalysts.
Photocatalytic degradation percentage of RhB and PhP in presence of as-prepared photocatalysts.
Position, FWHM of the prominent diffraction peaks, and the crystallite of as-prepared samples calculated from the XRD patterns.
Bandgap of as-prepared samples calculated from the UV–Vis spectra.
Comparison of photocatalytic efficiency of Z-CQD in this work with ZnO-CQDs and its Derivatives in earlier reports.

head.ce:title: Ball mill-hydrothermal method for one-step synthesis of zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (ZnO-CQD) nanocomposites as photocatalyst for degradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources and International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources and International Innovation Center for Forest Chemicals and Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, P.O. Box 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, P.O. Box 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Gilan, Rasht, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Gilan, Rasht, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, we report a cost-effective, green, one-step, and new method without any extra materials to prepare ZnO/CQD photocatalyst. Hence, ZnO nanoparticles and zinc oxide/carbon quantum dot (Z-CQD) nanocomposites were synthesized using zinc salt, KOH, and sucrose through the new green ball mill-hydrothermal method. The prepared nanomaterials were characterized by FT-IR, TEM, XRD, FE-SEM, TGA, and UV–Vis absorption. The analysis of the TGA result shows the presence of around 6 wt% of CQD in Z-CQD nanocomposite. The presence of CQD in ZnO nanoparticles decreased the bandgap. The photocatalytic performance of ZnO and Z-CQD were compared by evaluating the degradation of phenazopyridine (PhP), a drug pollutant, and rhodamine B (RhB), an organic dye pollutant, under UV light. The results showed that the degradation rate of RhB is higher than that of PhP. In addition, in the case of RhB, the degradation rate by Z-CQD is higher than that of pure ZnO. Therefore, the presence of carbon quantum dots showed a positive effect on the rate of photocatalytic degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, significant efforts have been made to develop innovative strategies to solve the issues related to the industrial discharge of hazardous materials, including dyes [1,2], heavy metals [3], toxic materials from textiles [4], pharmaceutical pollutants[5], etc. in wastewater which can cause an irreversible effect on the human health and marine life [6]. The main examples of these dangerous substances are organic dyes and heavy metals from textile industries and other industrial companies [7]. Thus, semiconductor photocatalysts such as ZnO and TiO2, two well-known photocatalysts, can be applied to eliminate the organic pollutant from wastewater [1,8,9]. ZnO is a suitable alternative for other semiconductors due to its bandgap (3.37 eV) [10], considerable exaction binding energy (60 meV) at room temperature [11], non-toxicity, modifiable, and chemical and mechanical stability [1,8,12]. There are various ways to improve the oxidation process using semiconductor photocatalysts, such as combining two semiconductors [13–16], modifying by applying metal and carbon nanoparticles, etc. [17]. One of the main strategies to improve the semiconductor’s performance is to combine semiconductor photocatalysts with carbon nanomaterials [18,19]. Composites based on carbon nanomaterials are ideal candidates for photocatalytic applications due to theirhighelectricalconductivity, high surface area, and low cost [20]. Carbon nanomaterials, especially carbon quantum dots (CQDs), have attracted the attention of many researchers due to their outstanding properties [21,22]. The CQDs improve the oxidation process of semiconductor photocatalysts [23] because of their unique properties such as up-conversion of photoluminescence properties, higher photo-induced electron transfer, biocompatibility, low toxicity, and low cost [23–25]. In previous researches, we synthesized ZnO nanostructures and ZnO/CNTs nanocomposites by the Ball mill-hydrothermal method and utilized photocatalysts activity for photodegradation of PhP (a typical pharmaceutical pollutant) and malachite green (MG, a typical organic dye pollutant), respectively (Table 1 ) [26,27].

In this work, for the first time, by eliminating extra steps and materials compared to other reported methods such as sol–gel, electrochemical, etc. for synthesizing of Z-CQDs, the Z-CQDs nanocomposites were synthesized through the Ball mill-hydrothermal method. Also, the photodegradation of PhP and RhB was investigated by prepared Z-CQD nanocomposites.

The reagents used for this research, namely anhydrous zinc dichloride powder (ZnCl2), sucrose, phenazopyridine hydrochloride (PhP), and Rhodamine B (RB) were supplied by Merck (Germany). potassium hydroxide (KOH, 85%) was obtained from Samchun Chemicals.The reagents were used as received and prepared using deionized water.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was recorded by the KBr disk method (Bruckermodel TENSOR 27, Germany) at 400–4000 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was done by (Bruker AXS D8 Discover diffractometer operating at 40 kV and 40 mA) with monochromatic CuKa (l = 1.5406 Å) radiation, Analytik Jena UV–vis spectrophotometer model Specord 250 (Germany) was applied to record the UV–vis absorption spectra of as-prepared samples. Samples were studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) (Tescan, Czech Republic) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Philips, Netherlands). Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was used to identify the composition of some samples. Thermal property of ZnO and Z-CQD samples was investigated using Thermogravimetric Analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesize of zinc oxide–carbon quantum dots nanocomposites (Z-CQD)
ce:section.ce:para: For the synthesis of zinc oxide–carbon quantum dots nanocomposites by ballmilling-hydrothermal method, first, different amounts of sucrose (5, 10, 15, and 20 wt%) were mixed with ZnCl2 (1.6 g) and KOH (5 g) in a ball mill container. The reaction mixture was milled for 2 h in a half-hour ball mill for 4 cycles, rinsed with 70 mL of deionized water and then poured into a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave and placed at 180 °C for 5 h. Finally, the resulting precipitate was washed several times with deionized water to neutralize and then dried at 80 °C for one day. The prepared nanocomposites were labeled as Z-CQDX, where X represents the weight percentage of sucrose compared to anhydrous zinc dichloride powder. In addition, for comparison, zinc oxide was synthesized under the same conditions (1.6 g ZnCl2, 5 g KOH, 2 h in a half-hour ball mill for 4 cycles, and hydrothermal at 180 °C for 5 h) in the absence of sucrose.

100 mg of zinc oxide nanostructure and Z-CQD nanocomposites were added to 100 mL of RhB and PhP dye solution (20 ppm) and stirred at room temperature. To investigate the adsorption equilibrium of RhB and PhP with the catalyst, first the reaction mixture was stirred in a dark environment for 30 min. The suspension was then irradiated with a 125 W mercury lamp (the most vigorous emission at 365 nm and 32 cm away from the suspension) for 80 min. After irradiating the suspension, 4 mL of the sample was removed at regular intervals (every 20 min) and centrifuged to remove the catalyst from the sample. The centrifuged sample was analyzed using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The photocatalytic activity of the synthesized samples was investigated according to the difference in the intensity of RhB at 560 nm and PhP at 431 nm, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the preparation mechanism of Z-CQD nanocomposites.The synthesis of nanocomposite was performed using the mechanochemical method and only by ZnCl2, KOH, and sucrose. This is a cost-effective and facile in-situ method without demanding complex equipment.

Fig. 2 shows FT-IR spectra of prepared ZnO, Z-CQD 5%, Z-CQD 10%, and 20%. The vibrational band in 558 corresponds to the zn-o stretching vibration [28]. The broad peak in 3400 cm−1 relates to the OH stretching vibration of the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the zinc oxide catalyst. The peaks at 2921 cm−1 and 2853 cm−1 are dedicated to CH stretching vibration[29]. The peaks around 1514 cm−1 and 1639 cm−1 are related to asymmetric and symmetrical OCO vibrations. The peaks around 1300 cm−1 are related to the CO band, and peaks associated with the epoxy group are presented around 900 cm−1 [30]. These peaks indicate a successful response between ZnO and CQD.

The approximate size (D) of the Z-CQD nanocomposite and pure zinc oxide was calculated using the Scherrer Eq. (1).
(1)
D =
K
λ
β
c o s ( θ
)
where K is the constant of the equation and equal to 0.9, λ is the scatter wavelength of X-ray and equal to 0.154 nm and β is the peak width at half the maximum height in radians, and θ is the scattering angle in degrees. Using Eq. (2), the peak width is obtained in radians.
(2)
β =
β ( d e g r e e ) × 2
π
360

Fig. 5 shows the field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) of ZnO and Z-CQD 20%. According to the limited resolution, CQDs cannot be observed [33]. SEM images show that by covering the nanoparticles with quantum dots, the previous morphology is maintained and only the size of the nanoparticles changes, and there is no size aggregation. Due to the low resolution of the SEM analysis, the Cds are not observable in SEM images.

The results of the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) of ZnO and Z-CQD 20% shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6 indicate the presence of zinc, oxygen (oxygen observed in the EDX spectrum is related to both zinc oxide and carbon quantum dot), and carbon, which strongly confirm the presence of CQD and ZnO phases in the Z-CQD composite [34]. There is no other signal in these patterns and this indicates the high purity of the samples synthesized by ball mill-hydrothermal method.

Fig. 7 shows the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) of ZnO and Z-CQD 20%. TEM image of 20% Z-CQD nanocomposite shows the presence of zinc oxide nanoparticles and carbon quantum dots. The diameter of the carbon quantum dots is less than 10 nm. It is observed that CQDs are scattered on the surface of ZnO nanoparticles and create heterogeneous, and uneven surfaces, and CQDs have very low density. In Fig. 7, part A, a heterogeneous and uneven surface is observed, which indicates the presence of carbon quantum dots, and the relatively smooth and uniform surface observed in part B indicates the absence of carbon quantum dots. The surface of ZnO nanoparticles seems softer and smoother than the surface where CQD sits on ZnO, which indicates that forming a carbon layer is due to adding the CQDs. the TEM images will be presented to prove the presence of these particles.

The optical properties of nanoparticles are more proposed about the semiconductors. In these materials, the optical properties are directly related to the particle size, so the intensity of the optical spectrum of the semiconductor's nanoparticles chang by changing their size due to the semiconductor's electronic structure [35]. In the material, there is an energy gap between the conduction band and the valence band, which is called the forbidden band. The valence band is full of electrons, and the energy of the forbidden band is high in the insulation materials. According to these conditions, it is not possible to transfer electrons from the valence band to the conduction band in insulation materials. In contrast, in conductive materials such as metals, the conduction band, and valance band overlap, so, it is easy to transfer the electron. In semiconductors, there is a forbidden band, but its energy is not high. Therefore, thermal energy can transfer some of the electrons to the conduction band even at room temperature and limited electrical conductivity is achieved. If photons are emitted into a semiconductor whose energy is higher than the energy of the forbidden band, the electrons are transferred to the conduction band and an electron-hole pair called an exciton is formed [36]. In nanoparticles, the particle size controls the distance between the electron-holes, so that as the particle size becomes proper for the nanometer scale, the motion of the exciton is limited, and a transfer is observed in the optical spectrum [37]. Fig. 8 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the prepared samples and the value of the energy band gap of Z-CQD nanocomposites is obtained through Eq. (3) and the results are summarized in Table 2 .
(3)
E g
=
hc
λ

h = 6.626×34-10 j.s is Plank constant and c = 3 × 108 m/s light speed and λ is wavelength in nm. Eg, is obtained in terms of joules, which is converted to eV. An absorbent peak was observed by passing through the bandgap in the UV area. The outline of the tangent line through the curvature points in the absorption spectrum and the energy axis of the photon specify the energy of the bandgap for pure ZnO and nanocomposite.

Based on the outline of the tangent line through the curvature point shown in Fig. 8, the synthesized Z-CQDs have a notable redshift compared to ZnO [38]. Therefore, a smaller band gap is observed in nanocomposites compared to pure zinc oxide, which indicates the ability of nanocomposites to absorb more. In UV, the obtained peaks have high absorption and this indicates the higher photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites compared to pure nanoparticles for the considered reaction under UV light.

The thermal stability of pure ZnO and Z-CQD nano-catalysts in the nitrogen atmosphere was investigated using TGA. The TGA of Z-ZCQD 20% nanocomposite and pure ZnO were performed from 25 °C to 700 °C using heating rate of 10 °C /min (Fig. 9 ). Fig. 9 shows that 86.97% of the original mass of Z-CQD 20 nanocomposite and about 95.12% of pure zinc oxide remain, which is related to the structure of nanoparticles. The main process of decomposition of Z-CQD 20% nanocomposite and ZnO destruction occurs in the range of 200 °C to 700 °C. According to the TGA analysis results, the number of carbon quantum dots is about 6.1% by weight in the nanocomposite.

The photocatalytic activity of zinc oxide and Z-CQD nanocomposites under UV radiation was investigated for the photocatalytic degradation of PhP as a pharmaceutical pollutant and RhB as a dye pollutant in water and wastewater. Fig. 10 shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of PhP and RB aqueous solutions in the presence of UV light and a constant amount of ZnO and Z-CQD nanocomposites at different times. Fig. 11 shows the comparison of the photocatalytic behavior of ZnO NPs and Z-CQD nanocomposites in dye and pharmaceutical pollutants under similar conditions. Based on previous works, under similar conditions, the blank material had very little destruction and we refused to repeat it again[26].The reqsults show a high percentage of degradation of RhB compared to PhP, which can be due to the stability of the PhP (Fig. 12 ). The percentage of degradation of drug and dye pollutant was calculated according to the following Eq. (4).
(4)
D e g r a d a t i o n
( % )
C o
-
C t
C o
× 100
A o
-
A t
A o
× 100

Co, Ao, Ct and At are the initial concentration and absorption of the pollutant, the concentration and absorption of the pollutant exposed to radiation after tmin, respectively. According to the results of these photocatalytic tests, it can be concluded that:
By modifying the surface of ZnO NPs by quantum dots, the amount of band gap energy is reduced compared to pure ZnO NPs and the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposite is more and better [39].

ZnO NPs absorb UV light to stimulate electron-hole pairs produced by light under UV light, in which CQDs act as an electron bath. The produced electrons are transferred from the ZnO surface to CQDs, and the recombination of the electron-hole pairs produced by the light can be effectively suppressed. Therefore, the present nanocomposite has more photocatalytic activity compared to pure zinc oxide.

When cavities are near the zinc oxide capacity band, it is possible to generate a large number of active optimal sites, which can help to produce high-energy free radicals. For example, strong electrons in carbon quantum dots and zinc oxide can react with absorbed oxygen molecules to produce superoxide radicals (O2
-). At the same time, the cavities migrate to the surface and react with water molecules and produce radical hydroxide (OH). Perhydroxide (OOH
) is then produced by the protonation of superoxide. In general, these high-energy free radicals are responsible for breaking down larger molecules into smaller ones[40]. Therefore, the superior photocatalytic activity of Z-CQD nanocomposites is mainly due to the combined effect of CQD on semiconductors[40–42]. The photocatalytic mechanism for contaminant destruction is according to Eqs. (5)–(11):
(5) Z-CQD + hν → e- (CQD) + h+ (ZnO)
(6) h+ + H 2 O → OH
+ H +
(7)
e - + O 2 → O 2
-
(8)
O 2
- + 2H + → H 2 O 2
(9)
OH 2
+ H2O → H2O2 + OH
(10) H2O2 → 2 OH
(11)
OH
+ Pollutant → CO2 + H2O

When Z-CQD is exposed to UV light with higher energy or ZnO bandgap, the electrons are excited from the valence layer or ZnO capacity to the CQD conduction band and create a hole (+h) on the ZnO capacity band. These cavities react with H2O to produce OH
. The generated electrons by light react with CQD oxygen and produce O2
-, which produces a strong oxidizing agent for pollutant decomposition from protonation of hydroperoxyradicalHO2
[43,44]. Table 3 illustrate the photocatalytic efficiency comparison of Z-CQD in this work with ZnO-CQDs and its derivatives in some earlier reports. As result, Table 3 shows that the as-synthesized ZnO-CQDs via ball mill-hydrothermal have a well photocatalytic efficiency, same as ZnO-CQDs in earlier reports.

In this research, zinc oxide nanostructures with spherical-rod morphology were successfully synthesized only with two precursors (anhydrous zinc dichloride plus KOH) using a newly developed ball milling-hydrothermal method. Subsequently, Z-CQD nanocomposites were successfully synthesized using different amounts of sucrose as the carbon source and then used for the photodegradation of drug and color contaminants. Interaction between the surface of ZnO and carbon quantum dots in Z-CQD improved the photocatalytic activity of nanocomposites compared to pure zinc oxide. The increase of carbon quantum dots to zinc nanoparticles increased the percentage of drug and dye removal. Also, the XRD analysis revealed that the size of nanoparticles is decreased by increasing the amount of carbon quantum dots. Based on the proposed mechanism and the obtained results, the synthesis of Z-CQDs composites through hydrothermal ball milling has advantages, including:
1)
Unlike the other reported methods for preparing Z-CQDs nanocomposites, in the Ball mill-Hydrothermal method, the Z-CQDs nanocomposites will be synthesis directly (not doping or coupling CQD on ZnO or vice versa).
2)
Elimination of extra steps and additional materials (one step and only three precursors).
3)
Completely green and safe mechanochemical method.
Therefore, the new green synthetic method of ball milling-hydrothermal is a proper method for preparing this nanocomposite.

Sina Bazazi: Data curation. Saeedeh Jodeyri: Investigation. Seyedeh Parisa Hosseini: Investigation. Nasser Arsalani: Investigation. Bahaaldin Rashidzadeh: Supervision. Soghra Fathalipour: Writing – review & editing. Farzad Seidi: Data curation. Esmail Hashemi: Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China, and the University of Tabriz, Iran.
